risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Patients were randomly assigned to either groups – MBSR group or TAU group – using computer-generated random tables." Comment: This explicitly describes a proper random allocation method (computer-generated random tables), which is sufficient to judge random sequence generation as low risk.

_evidence.method_quote: Patients were randomly assigned to either groups – MBSR group or TAU group – using computer-generated random tables.
_evidence.method_summary: Participants were assigned to groups using computer-generated random tables.

risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Patients were randomly assigned to either groups – MBSR group or TAU group – using computer-generated random tables." Comment: This describes sequence generation, but the article does not state any concealment mechanism (e.g., central allocation, sequentially numbered opaque sealed envelopes, or identical containers) that would prevent the recruiter from foreseeing the next assignment. The extracted evidence explicitly notes that concealment was "not described," so allocation concealment remains unclear.

_evidence.note: The article states that patients were randomly assigned using computer-generated random tables, but it does not describe how the allocation sequence was concealed from the recruiter.

risk_of_bias.domain: Blinding of participants and personnel (performance bias)
risk_of_bias.support_text: Summary: The extracted evidence says participants and personnel were not blinded (“participants_blinded": "no", "personnel_blinded": "no"; blinding method: "none described; intervention was MBSR vs treatment-as-usual, with a health education session for both groups"). The study also assessed multiple subjective outcomes, including HADS anxiety/depression, perceived stress, meditation compliance, and adverse effects. Comment: Because blinding was absent and several dominant outcomes were patient-reported/subjective, knowledge of allocation could plausibly influence the results across the study overall.

_evidence.blinding_method: none described; intervention was MBSR vs treatment-as-usual, with a health education session for both groups

risk_of_bias.support_text: Summary: The outcome assessor was mixed: patient self-report for HADS anxiety/depression/total score and perceived stress (PSS), with objective measures for systolic BP and BMI. The article states, "The Hospital Anxiety and Depression Scale (HADS) was used to assess anxiety and depressive symptoms in patients with CHD" and "Perceived stress was assessed using the Perceived Stress Scale (PSS)." It also states BP was measured "by the cardiologist or a physician." Comment: Because the study includes patient-reported outcomes, the patient is effectively the assessor for those outcomes. With no evidence that participants were blinded, knowledge of allocation could influence reporting of anxiety, depression, and stress. Under the worst-outcome rule, these subjective outcomes make the domain High risk even though BP and BMI are objective.

Quote: "Of the 30 patients, there were a total of 17 available at 3-month follow-up (12 in the MBSR and five in the TAU group)." Comment: This gives explicit post-randomization attrition on the analysed follow-up outcome: 3/15 lost in MBSR (20%) versus 10/15 lost in TAU (66.7%), a very large differential loss of 46.7 percentage points and total loss of 43.3%. Reasons for non-attendance were not described, and the analysis was complete-case, so the reported attrition could plausibly bias the follow-up results.

per_arm_flow.reasons: At 3-month follow-up, 12 in the MBSR group were available out of 15; reasons for non-attendance not described. "As some participants could not attend scheduled sessions for intervention or post-assessment as per the appointment date, they were given appointment for the alternate week"
At 3-month follow-up, five in the TAU group were available out of 15; reasons for non-attendance not described.

_evidence.notes: Attrition numbers are explicitly reported for the 3-month follow-up analysis; post-intervention numbers are not separately given. The article states: "Of the 30 patients, there were a total of 17 available at 3-month follow-up (12 in the MBSR and five in the TAU group)."
